summary: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) supplies all intrinsic and extrinsic tongue muscles except palatoglossus.
The hypoglossal nerve supplies only the intrinsic muscles of the tongue.
The glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) supplies all the motor innervation to the tongue.
When the hypoglossal nerve is damaged on one side, the tongue deviates toward the affected side on protrusion.
When the hypoglossal nerve is damaged, the tongue deviates away from the affected side.
Hypoglossal nerve damage causes the tongue to remain in the midline.
Taste sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is carried by the chorda tympani (CN VII) via the lingual nerve.
Taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is carried by the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX).
The lingual nerve carries taste fibres directly without any contribution from CN VII.
The lateral border of the tongue is the most common site for oral squamous cell carcinoma in India.
The tip of the tongue is the most common site for oral cancer.
The dorsum of the tongue is the most common site for oral cancer.
The sulcus terminalis is a V-shaped groove that separates the anterior two-thirds from the posterior one-third of the tongue.
The sulcus terminalis separates the left and right halves of the tongue.
The sulcus terminalis is a ridge on the inferior surface of the tongue.
